names.original: Theologische Erklaerung zur gegenwaertigen Lage der Deutschen Evangelischen Kirche

date.note: Adopted by the first Confessing Synod of the German Evangelical Church at Barmen (Wuppertal-Barmen), 29-31 May 1934.

summary: The Theological Declaration of Barmen (May 1934) is the founding confessional statement of the German Confessing Church (Bekennende Kirche), drafted principally by Karl Barth. In six theses it confesses Jesus Christ as the one Word of God whom the church must hear and obey, and rejects the accommodation of the church to the ideology of the 'German Christian' movement and the Fuehrer principle.
description:
The Theological Declaration of Barmen was adopted by the first Confessing Synod of the German Evangelical Church, meeting at Barmen in the Ruhr from 29 to 31 May 1934, at the height of the church struggle (Kirchenkampf) under National Socialism. It was drafted principally by the Reformed theologian Karl Barth, together with the Lutherans Hans Asmussen and Thomas Breit and others, and became the founding confessional text of the Confessing Church (Bekennende Kirche), the movement that resisted the attempt to bring the church into line (Gleichschaltung) with Nazi ideology and the pro-Nazi 'German Christian' (Deutsche Christen) movement.

The declaration is built of six theses, each pairing a scriptural affirmation with a corresponding rejection ('We reject the false doctrine...'). Its first thesis, prefaced with John 14:6 and John 10:1,9, confesses: 'Jesus Christ, as he is attested to us in Holy Scripture, is the one Word of God whom we have to hear, and whom we have to trust and obey in life and in death.' Its rejection clause then states: 'We reject the false doctrine that the church could and should recognize as a source of its proclamation, beyond and besides this one Word of God, yet other events, powers, historic figures and truths, as God's revelation.' The remaining theses confess that Jesus Christ is God's claim upon the whole of life (Thesis 2), that the church's order and message must not be handed over to prevailing ideological and political convictions (Thesis 3), that ministry in the church is service and not domination or the exercise of lordship (Thesis 4), that the state has its appointed but limited task and must not become the single and totalitarian order of human life (Thesis 5), and that the church's commission is the free proclamation of grace and is not to be placed in the service of any arbitrary human plan (Thesis 6). Taken together the theses ground the church's proclamation and order in Jesus Christ alone and refuse any second source of revelation drawn from the events, powers, and figures of the age.

Barmen did not name Hitler or the National Socialist state explicitly; its rejections are framed as points of doctrine. Its concentration of revelation in Jesus Christ, above all in Thesis 1, is continuous with the christocentric shape of Barth's theology, and the declaration became the confessional charter of the Confessing Church and a reference point for later Reformed and ecumenical confession. This entry records Barmen as the historical confession of the Confessing Church, attributed to that body, and does not adjudicate the theological disputes between the Confessing Church and its opponents.
editorial_notes:
This entry describes Barmen on the tradition's own terms (neutrality #1, #4, #6) and attributes its rejections to the Confessing Church as the historical confession they were. The declaration's opposition to the 'German Christian' movement's accommodation of the church to Nazi ideology and the Fuehrer principle is stated as attributed historical fact (the anti-Nazi character of the Confessing Church's stand is uncontroversial historical record), not editorialized, and the intra-church theological disputes are not adjudicated.

HONESTY / status and confidence: this entry is REVIEWED. The load-bearing quotations (Thesis 1 and its rejection clause) rest on the Barmen text itself, an OPEN, short confessional document read directly for this entry via the United Church of Christ text; that primary source is rated HIGH (verified-eligible). Wording of the quoted passages follows the UCC English translation; other English translations (for example the Book of Confessions of the Presbyterian Church USA) render the same clauses with minor verbal differences, noted here for transparency. The scholarly sources are the standard biography by Barth's assistant Eberhard Busch and Arthur Cochrane's monograph on the church struggle and the Barmen text, both verified to exist and be on-topic but not read line-by-line; they are rated MEDIUM. The three Christian lenses (neutrality #8): (a) academic historians of the Confessing Church and of Barth (Cochrane; Busch); (b) the primary corpus (the Barmen Declaration 1934, read); (c) a contemporary theologian of Barth (Busch also serves here, and Barth as principal drafter is himself the theological voice). No encyclopedias, no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources.

relations.note: Drafted principally by Karl Barth, with Hans Asmussen, Thomas Breit, and others, as a collective synodal document to which Barth was the chief contributor. karl-barth exists in the dataset and references this work.

Adopted by the first Confessing Synod at Barmen (29-31 May 1934) and became the founding confessional statement of the Confessing Church (Bekennende Kirche). Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.
A twentieth-century confessional statement of the German Evangelical (Protestant) churches, received across Reformed, Lutheran, and United bodies. protestantism exists in the dataset.
Drafted principally by the Reformed theologian Karl Barth and later incorporated among the confessional documents of Reformed and united churches. reformed-tradition exists in the dataset.
Confesses Jesus Christ as the one Word of God whom the church must hear and obey, grounding the church's proclamation christologically; trinity exists in the dataset.

sources.citation: The Theological Declaration of Barmen, 1934. English translation, Theses 1-6, including Thesis 1 ('Jesus Christ, as he is attested to us in Holy Scripture, is the one Word of God whom we have to hear, and whom we have to trust and obey in life and in death') prefaced by John 14:6 and John 10:1,9, and its rejection clause ('We reject the false doctrine that the church could and should recognize as a source of its proclamation, beyond and besides this one Word of God, yet other events, powers, historic figures and truths, as God's revelation'). Read directly for this entry; the primary confessional text and load-bearing source for the quoted passages.
sources.license: Confessional text of 1934; the quoted passages are short and attributed. Open and publicly available.

sources.citation: Cochrane, Arthur C. The Church's Confession Under Hitler. Philadelphia: Westminster Press, 1962. Monograph on the German church struggle and the Confessing Church that includes an English translation and analysis of the Barmen Declaration; cited as the academic-historical lens on the declaration and its context. Verified to exist and be on-topic, not read line-by-line.

Busch, Eberhard. Karl Barth: His Life from Letters and Autobiographical Texts. Translated by John Bowden. Philadelphia: Fortress Press, 1976. The standard biography by Barth's last assistant, drawn from his correspondence and papers, covering the church struggle and Barth's role as principal drafter of Barmen; cited as a second academic-biographical lens and, given Barth's authorship, a witness to the theological voice behind the declaration. Verified to exist and be on-topic, not read line-by-line.
